items.mistake: Counting library calls as O(1) without checking.
items.fix: Look up the complexity of every hot-path call (e.g. `x in list` is O(n), `x in set` is O(1)).
items.why: Hidden loops in 'one-liner' calls are the leading cause of accidental O(n²).
items.example:
```python
# Looks O(n), is actually O(n*m) because `in` on a list is O(m):
result = [w for w in words if w in stopword_list]
```

items.mistake: Confusing worst-case with average or amortized cost.
items.fix: State which case you mean. For Python lists, `append` is **amortized** O(1) but a single resize is O(n).
items.why: Saying 'dict lookup is O(1)' is shorthand for *average* O(1); under adversarial keys it is O(n).
items.example:
```python
lst = []
for i in range(N): lst.append(i)   # amortized O(1) per append
```

items.mistake: Adding constants and lower-order terms 'to be precise'.
items.fix: Drop them. O(3n² + 100n + 5) and O(n²) are the same statement.
items.why: Big-O is about asymptotic growth; constants and lower-order terms vanish at large n.

items.mistake: Confusing time complexity with space complexity.
items.fix: Track both separately; an algorithm can be O(n) time but O(n²) space (e.g. some DP solutions).
items.why: A fast algorithm that won't fit in RAM is no faster than the slow one.
items.example:
```python
# n × n DP table: O(n²) space even though each cell is O(1) to fill.
import numpy as np
dp = np.zeros((n, n))
```

items.mistake: Reading 'O(n)' as 'n operations' instead of 'grows linearly with n'.
items.fix: Big-O is a shape, not a count. 10n is also O(n).
items.why: Two O(n) algorithms can differ by 100× in wall-clock time on the same input.
items.example:
```python
# Both O(n) but the second has a much larger constant:
for x in xs: pass                       # ~ 1 unit per element
for x in xs: complicated_call(x)         # ~ 100 units per element
```
